dom.title: Pebble+ Edit Workbook
dom.contentPreview: University Quality Procedures. When a new course is proposed it is scrutinised by committee to see if it aligns with the national standards for higher education. Each module is reviewed by students at the end of each block of teaching. 10% of all assignments are second marked and the staff have a benchmarking meeting before marking begins. 6 assignments from each module are sent to an external examiner from another university to check the feedback and standards. These results, as well as data from the grades and submissions student's make, are reviewed by a programme committee. The marks and statistics from the modules go to exam board where they are ratified and the external examiner will feedback on the course and assignments. At periodic intervals, the university will review courses and check they still match their proposed learning intentions and module specifications. Extensions are only granted in certain situations. These are not granted by tutors. Please contact infopoint@uwe.ac.uk and book an appointment with a student advisor. PROGRESS TRACKING Mark page as complete View progress

dom.contentPreview: Learning and teaching terminology - Academic information | UWE Bristol New resources to support assessments with critical questioning Colleagues in SHSW have worked with the CHSS Library team to produce three videos to support students to prepare for their assessment. You are free to use or adapt them for your students as appropriate. For context, the assessment is detailed below: Assessment outline: An evidence-based poster relating to a change proposal for service improvement that will include rationale and drivers; evidence base; change management approach using a change model of choice, inclusive of the anticipated leadership approach and critical debate. Supported by a 5-minute presentation in which to demonstrate leadership and influencing ability. Accompanied by 10 minutes of critical questioning to ascertain wide reading and knowledge of leadership models and approaches in relation to change management theory. The resources, which we believe have wider appeal, are: Video advice and support for your assessment: Assessment overview and top tips - https://vimeo.com/1008094667 What to expect during critical questioning - https://vimeo.com/1008095123 How to do well during critic‚Ä¶

dom.contentPreview: Generative AI Generative AI (Artificial Intelligence) has many applications and can be a useful a tool for your studies. However, you need to be careful when using AI for your assessment. If you do not reference or attribute AI content appropriately, this could be seen as an assessment offence. For further information speak to your tutor about the appropriate use of AI in specific assessments. Here are two UWE Bristol guides to using AI in your studies and assessment: This guide suggests ways to use AI effectively in your studies: Generative AI study skills guide - Advice and guidance on how to make the most of generative artificial intelligence (AI). This guide is about using AI in your assessment - including how to attribute and reference AI content appropriately to avoid assessment offences: Using generative AI at UWE Bristol - A guide to when and how to use generative AI. You are permitted to use Gen AI tools in an assistive role in this assessment to support you with ‚Äã Editing original content and structuring original content;‚Äã supporting a particular process such as translating content;‚Äã giving your feedback on your clarity, coherence and level of criticality.‚Äã Please note th‚Ä¶

Activity In this unit, we have been reflecting on values and how this impacts on our approach to teaching. To get you to start to focus on the values of other teachers and pupils' learning we are going to get you to do some observation of teaching and learning. The activity will prepare you for the next unit on the developing pupil and to start thinking about reflecting on your own teaching. The video below introduces the focused observation. Choose one of the observation sheets and carry out an observation of a single pupil for about 30 mins in a colleague's lesson or monitor a pupil in your own lesson. You don't have to interact with the pupil. Please ask the teacher's permission before carrying this out. Respond to the focus questions below. 1.What values do you think the teacher holds from your observation? dom.contentPreview: Unit 2 Step 1 What do I think about learning? This unit ask you to consider how you think children learn. It explores some learning theories and asks you to analyse some teaching to reflect on the learning theories that underpin the actions of the teacher. ‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã Learning Outcomes: To demonstrate a critical, evidence-based understanding of learner development Activity This activity is designed to start you thinking about how you believe pupils learn. Read the statements below. Sort them into those you agree with, those you don‚Äôt and those you are not sure about. Put them on the Padlet in the three columns in your own colour and look at what others think. Are they the same as yours? Why do you think teachers have different views on learning ? These notes will start your thinking on your presentation and written assignment. Statements about learning: Pupils are empty containers that need to be filled with knowledge. Pupils learn best when they understand what the learning is for. Pupils need to learn from each other as that is the way they will work in the future If you repeat something enough times pupils will learn it. Some pupils are just born with less intelligence than others, ‚Ä¶

dom.contentPreview: Unit 5 Step 4 Auditing our own Teaching and Questioning. Learning Outcomes Evaluate their own personal values, beliefs and experiences and consider the implications of these in their roles as educator Activity Questioning can be the most valuable strategy you use in the classroom. It can encourage pupils to think more critically, identify diverse ideas, extend their thinking and help them see different ways to proceed. 1. Audit one of your own lessons. You could do this by recording yourself on your phone ( if school allows phones in classroom). Be sure to delete after use and to record your voice and not that of the pupils. Consider: How many open/closed questions did you use ‚Äì is the balance right? Are you playing guess what the teacher is thinking of? What do you do with incorrect responses ‚Äì move on from, ignore, develop or ridicule? Can you employ some of the lessons learnt in this topic? Set yourself a target to work on in the future: For example; Scaffold answers for weaker pupils Utilise a hinge questions (see below) Increase take up/thinking time. Make notes on your experiences of your questioning below and bring to the next live session. Notes on Audit Notes on Audit mult‚Ä¶

dom.title: Pebble+ Edit Workbook
dom.contentPreview: Recording your Teaching UWE Students' Agreement for using Panopto to Record Practice What is Panopto? As part of our student development process, we use Panopto software to enable you to securely record parts of your professional practice. The recordings are stored in a secure cloud environment which you may access at any time during the course. You will share the recordings with UWE tutors to help support your learning and development. You can use it: To focus one element of your practice following an agreed focus (e.g. behaviour management); To record a whole or part of a session where you want to demonstrate your progress, skills, knowledge, and understanding; If you have a particular problem which you want to share and gather feedback and/or discussion on that particular concern. All recordings are for your professional development only. The videos are only to be shared as agreed with UWE. This means that only your university tutor will ever have need of viewing your videos, or (on occasion) with your agreement with other students in your group to demonstrate good practice. Before using Panopto: In order to comply with GDPR and other data processing regulations please familaris‚Ä¶

Follow the above link to explore what people mean when they talk about referencing and plagiarism, as well as why and how we reference. You'll also have the opportunity to practise: following citing and referencing rules to acknowledge your sources and avoid plagiarism applying the best fit approach to different types of information identifying effective paraphrasing 'How to Plan and Structure Your Writing' workbook. This workbook will help develop your confidence in: Analysing your assignment title Organising your thoughts Structuring paragraphs Critical writing Thinking and writing critically involves asking the question: "What do I think about this - and why?" It means thinking analytically in order to explain how much you agree or disagree with something: it involves assessing the accuracy, usefulness and validity of what you read, hear or see. The aim of this workbook is to help you to develop critical thinking, reading and writing skills PROGRESS TRACKING Mark page as complete View progress
